sections.heading: What is Dependency Injection?
sections.content: Dependency Injection (DI) is a design pattern where an object receives its dependencies from an external source rather than creating them itself. Instead of writing val repository = UserRepository(UserDao(), ApiService()) inside a ViewModel, the ViewModel simply declares what it needs and a DI framework provides it automatically. This makes code more modular, testable, and maintainable — you can swap real implementations for fakes in tests without changing the classes being tested.

sections.heading: Why Dependency Injection?
items: Testability — inject mock or fake dependencies in unit tests without changing production code
Loose coupling — classes declare what they need, not how to create it, reducing tight interdependencies
Single responsibility — classes focus on their own logic, not on constructing their dependencies
Reusability — the same dependency can be injected into multiple classes without duplication
Lifecycle management — DI frameworks manage object creation, scoping, and destruction automatically
Scalability — as the app grows, managing manual dependency graphs becomes unmanageable; DI scales cleanly
Centralized configuration — all dependency wiring is in one place (Hilt modules), making changes easy
Google recommendation — Hilt is the officially recommended DI solution for Android apps

rows.manual_di: You manually construct and pass dependencies through constructors
rows.hilt: Hilt automatically constructs and injects dependencies at the right time

rows.manual_di: High — every class needs a factory or manual wiring
rows.hilt: Low — annotations handle wiring; Hilt generates the boilerplate

rows.manual_di: Manual — you manage object lifetime yourself
rows.hilt: Automatic — scopes like Singleton, ActivityScoped, ViewModelScoped handled by Hilt

rows.manual_di: Possible but requires extra effort to swap dependencies
rows.hilt: First-class — use @TestInstallIn or hiltRule to replace modules in tests

rows.manual_di: Requires a custom ViewModelFactory for every ViewModel
Becomes unmanageable as the app grows
rows.hilt: Scales well — Hilt manages the entire dependency graph

sections.heading: What is Hilt?
sections.content: Hilt is Google's officially recommended dependency injection library for Android, built on top of Dagger 2. It reduces the boilerplate of Dagger by providing standard components and scopes that are pre-built for Android's component hierarchy — Application, Activity, Fragment, View, Service, and BroadcastReceiver. Hilt uses annotation processing to generate all the dependency wiring code at compile time, so there is no reflection overhead at runtime. The result is a DI setup that is type-safe, performant, and deeply integrated with Jetpack components like ViewModel and Navigation.

sections.heading: Setting Up Hilt
items: Step 1: Add the Hilt Gradle plugin to the project-level build.gradle.kts plugins block: id("com.google.dagger.hilt.android") version "2.x" apply false
Step 2: Apply the plugin in the app-level build.gradle.kts: id("com.google.dagger.hilt.android")
Step 3: Enable kapt or KSP in the app-level build.gradle.kts: id("org.jetbrains.kotlin.kapt") or id("com.google.devtools.ksp")
Step 4: Add Hilt dependencies: implementation("com.google.dagger:hilt-android:2.x") and kapt("com.google.dagger:hilt-android-compiler:2.x")
Step 5: Annotate your Application class with @HiltAndroidApp
Step 6: Annotate Android classes that need injection with @AndroidEntryPoint (Activities, Fragments, Services)
Step 7: Annotate ViewModels with @HiltViewModel and use @Inject constructor
Step 8: Create @Module classes with @InstallIn to provide dependencies Hilt cannot construct automatically

rows.purpose: Triggers Hilt code generation and sets up the application-level dependency container
Enables field injection into the annotated Android class
Marks the ViewModel for Hilt injection — no custom factory needed
Tells Hilt how to provide or inject a dependency
Contains provider methods (@Provides or @Binds) for dependencies Hilt cannot auto-create
Specifies which Hilt component this module's dependencies are available in
Tells Hilt how to create an instance of a type it cannot construct (e.g., Retrofit, Room)
Tells Hilt which implementation to use when an interface is injected
Scopes the dependency to the application lifetime — only one instance is ever created
Scopes the dependency to the Activity lifetime — new instance per Activity
Scopes the dependency to the ViewModel lifetime — tied to the ViewModel's lifecycle

sections.heading: @Inject Constructor — The Simplest Way to Inject
sections.content: The simplest way to make a class injectable with Hilt is to annotate its constructor with @Inject. This tells Hilt that it knows how to create this class and can inject it wherever it is requested. Hilt will automatically provide all the parameters of the annotated constructor, as long as it also knows how to provide those parameter types. This works for plain Kotlin classes like Repositories and UseCases that have no special construction requirements.

sections.heading: @Inject Constructor — How It Works
items: Annotate the primary constructor: class UserRepository @Inject constructor(private val dao: UserDao, private val api: UserApiService)
Hilt sees @Inject and knows it can create UserRepository by providing its constructor parameters
Hilt must also know how to provide UserDao and UserApiService — either via @Inject or @Provides in a module
No factory class is needed — Hilt generates all the wiring code at compile time
The class can then be injected into a ViewModel: class UserViewModel @Inject constructor(private val repo: UserRepository) : ViewModel()
Combined with @HiltViewModel: Hilt automatically wires the ViewModel factory — no ViewModelFactory needed
Only one @Inject constructor is allowed per class — multiple constructors are not supported
Fields and methods can also be annotated with @Inject for field injection in Android classes (Activities, Fragments)

rows.provides: Manually provides an instance by writing the construction logic in the function body
rows.binds: Tells Hilt which concrete implementation to use for an interface — no function body

rows.provides: For third-party classes (Retrofit, OkHttp, Room) that you cannot annotate with @Inject
rows.binds: When you have an interface and want to bind it to a concrete implementation

rows.provides: Can be in a regular object or class annotated with @Module
rows.binds: Must be in an abstract class annotated with @Module

rows.provides: Has a body that returns the constructed instance
rows.binds: Is abstract — no body; Hilt handles the binding

rows.provides: Slightly more overhead — executes construction code
rows.binds: More efficient — Hilt resolves at compile time with no runtime cost

sections.heading: Hilt Components and @InstallIn
items: Every Hilt module must declare which component it is installed in using @InstallIn
SingletonComponent — dependencies live as long as the Application; use for app-wide singletons like Retrofit and Room
ActivityComponent — dependencies live as long as the Activity; new instance per Activity
FragmentComponent — dependencies live as long as the Fragment
ViewModelComponent — dependencies live as long as the ViewModel; use @ViewModelScoped here
ServiceComponent — dependencies scoped to a Service lifetime
ActivityRetainedComponent — survives configuration changes like a ViewModel; use for Activity-scoped singletons
ViewComponent — scoped to a single View; rarely used

rows.use_for: Retrofit, OkHttpClient, Room Database, app-wide Repositories
Dependencies shared between Activity and ViewModel
Dependencies used only within a specific ViewModel
Dependencies tied to one Activity instance
Dependencies tied to one Fragment instance
Dependencies used only within a Service

sections.heading: Providing Third-Party Dependencies with @Provides
sections.content: Many dependencies in an Android app come from third-party libraries like Retrofit, OkHttp, Gson, or Room. You cannot add @Inject to these classes because you don't own their source code. Instead, you write a Hilt Module — a class annotated with @Module and @InstallIn — that contains @Provides functions. Each @Provides function tells Hilt how to construct one dependency by writing the construction code explicitly. Hilt calls these functions when a dependency is needed and caches the result based on the declared scope.

sections.heading: Typical Hilt Module Structure
items: @Module marks the class as a Hilt module containing dependency providers
@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) means all providers in this module live at the Application scope
@Provides @Singleton fun provideDatabase(@ApplicationContext context: Context): AppDatabase — provides Room database singleton
@Provides fun provideUserDao(db: AppDatabase): UserDao = db.userDao() — extracts DAO from the database
@Provides @Singleton fun provideRetrofit(): Retrofit — creates and configures the Retrofit instance
@Provides @Singleton fun provideApiService(retrofit: Retrofit): UserApiService = retrofit.create(UserApiService::class.java)
Hilt automatically resolves the parameter chain — provideApiService receives the Retrofit from provideRetrofit
Use @ApplicationContext to inject the application Context: fun provide(..., @ApplicationContext context: Context)

rows.purpose: Distinguishes between two providers that return the same type
Type-safe alternative to @Named — define a custom annotation instead of using a string
Injects the application-level Context — available in SingletonComponent
Injects the Activity-level Context — available in ActivityComponent and below

sections.heading: Field Injection in Android Classes
items: Activities and Fragments annotated with @AndroidEntryPoint support field injection
Declare the field with @Inject: @Inject lateinit var analyticsHelper: AnalyticsHelper
Hilt injects the field before onCreate() in Activities and before onAttach() in Fragments
Field-injected properties must be var and lateinit — they cannot be val or have private visibility
Avoid field injection in plain Kotlin classes — use constructor injection instead for better testability
Field injection is necessary for Android framework classes (Activity, Fragment, BroadcastReceiver) where you don't control the constructor
Never inject into a non-@AndroidEntryPoint class using field injection — it will silently not work
ViewModel classes use constructor injection (@HiltViewModel + @Inject constructor) — not field injection

rows.description: @HiltViewModel class UserViewModel @Inject constructor(private val repo: UserRepository) : ViewModel()
private val viewModel: UserViewModel by viewModels() — Hilt provides the factory automatically
class UserRepository @Inject constructor(private val dao: UserDao, private val api: UserApiService)
@Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object AppModule with @Provides functions for Room and Retrofit

sections.heading: Hilt for Testing
items: Use @HiltAndroidTest on test classes to enable Hilt injection in Android instrumented tests
Add HiltAndroidRule as a JUnit test rule to manage the Hilt component lifecycle in tests
Replace production modules with test modules using @TestInstallIn(components, replaces)
@TestInstallIn replaces the real module entirely — every test in the module gets the fake dependency
Use @UninstallModules on individual test classes to replace only specific tests' modules
For unit tests, use constructor injection — pass fake implementations directly in the constructor
FakeUserRepository implementing UserRepository interface can be injected in place of the real one
Hilt's testing support eliminates the need for manual dependency graphs in instrumented tests

rows.fix: Every Hilt app must have exactly one @HiltAndroidApp Application class — without it, Hilt won't work at all
Without @AndroidEntryPoint, Hilt cannot inject into the class — @Inject fields will remain null

rows.mistake: Using @Inject on a field with private visibility
rows.fix: Field-injected properties must be at least package-private — use lateinit var without private

rows.mistake: Providing the same type twice without qualifiers
rows.fix: Use @Named or a custom @Qualifier annotation to distinguish between two providers of the same type

rows.mistake: Injecting Activity Context in a Singleton-scoped provider
rows.fix: Use @ApplicationContext for singleton-scoped providers — Activity context would leak the Activity

rows.mistake: Not scoping expensive objects like Retrofit or Room Database
rows.fix: Always annotate app-wide singletons with @Singleton — without it, a new instance is created on every injection

rows.mistake: Using @InstallIn(ActivityComponent) for a Repository used by a ViewModel
rows.fix: Repositories should be in SingletonComponent — ViewModels outlive Activities in configuration changes

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Hilt transforms Android dependency injection from a complex, boilerplate-heavy Dagger setup into a streamlined, annotation-driven system that integrates naturally with Android's component hierarchy. By annotating Application with @HiltAndroidApp, Android classes with @AndroidEntryPoint, and ViewModels with @HiltViewModel, and by providing third-party dependencies through @Module classes, Hilt automatically wires your entire dependency graph at compile time. The result is cleaner, more testable, and more maintainable code where every class receives exactly what it needs without knowing how it was created.
